planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Vanhoutte spiraea blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering to avoid removing the following year's flower buds.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Vanhoutte Spiraea, commonly called Bridal Wreath, is one of the most beloved flowering shrubs in American horticulture — a large, wide-arching shrub that in April and May (zone 7–8) produces such a profusion of small, pure-white flower clusters along the entire length of each cascading branch that the branches appear covered with a white veil, creating the bridal wreath effect that gives the plant its common name. The arching, fountain-like form with flowers distributed along every branch tip is one of the most graceful and spectacular spring flowering shrub effects available in the northern and mid-South garden. Vanhoutte spiraea is a hybrid of Spiraea cantoniensis and S. trilobata that has been cultivated since the 1860s — more than 150 years of continuous popularity testify to its landscape merit. Unlike summer-blooming Japanese spiraea (S. japonica) cultivars that bloom on new wood, Vanhoutte blooms on old wood and must be pruned immediately after flowering — never in fall or winter, which would remove the following year's flower buds. In zone 9's heat, Vanhoutte performs best in zone 8 and colder; in the hottest parts of zone 9a, the summer heat can stress the plant.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Goldflame develops its best foliage coloration in full sun — the spring bronze-orange new growth becomes more vivid and the summer-to-fall transition colors more intense in direct sun than in shade.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Goldflame is the premier tricolor Japanese spiraea cultivar — producing a remarkable three-season foliage color progression that provides more sustained ornamental interest than almost any other compact shrub. The new spring growth emerges in a vivid bronze-orange to copper-red color that is one of the most striking spring colors available on any deciduous shrub; through summer the foliage matures to yellow-gold; in late summer and fall the leaves develop orange and red fall tones. The transition from bronze-orange spring → yellow-gold summer → orange-red fall provides effectively continuous color change interest from April through frost. In early summer, small rosy-pink flower clusters add additional color above the evolving foliage. Goldflame's compact size (2–3 feet, slightly wider) makes it proportionate for typical residential borders and it has become one of the most widely planted compact deciduous shrubs in American landscaping. Like other Japanese spiraea cultivars, it is extremely adaptable and tough — tolerating clay, urban conditions, drought, and the heat of zone 9 with minimal care. Shear lightly after the main bloom flush in early summer to stimulate vigorous new growth with fresh bronze-orange coloration.

common_name: Little Lime Hydrangea (Compact Lime-Green Panicle Hydrangea)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Little Lime stays 3–5 feet — significantly smaller than the full-size Limelight hydrangea (6–8 feet) — making it practical for typical residential borders.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Little Lime is the compact version of the celebrated Limelight hydrangea — a dwarf selection (3–5 feet) that produces the same distinctive chartreuse-to-cream flower panicles in a proportionate size for smaller residential garden spaces where the full-size Limelight (6–8 feet) would dominate. The flower color progression is identical to Limelight: opening chartreuse-green in midsummer, maturing to creamy white, and transitioning through pink to dusty rose and finally buff-parchment as the flowers age through summer into fall — providing a 4–5 month evolving color display from a single set of flower heads. Like all Hydrangea paniculata cultivars, Little Lime blooms on new wood and is pruned hard in late winter (to 12–18 inches) to encourage vigorous new growth and maximum flower production. This makes it essentially foolproof — unlike French hydrangeas that lose flowers if pruned at the wrong time, Little Lime reliably flowers on whatever new wood it produces after any pruning regime. In zone 9, morning sun with afternoon shade is preferred — sufficient sun for good flower production with heat protection during the intense Gulf South afternoon. The compact size makes Little Lime suitable for foundation plantings, smaller landscape spaces, and containers.

common_name: Red Drift Rose (Low-Growing Red Groundcover Rose)

planting.note: Plant container roses in late winter through spring in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile, loamy soil in full sun; Red Drift requires at least 6 hours of direct sun daily for best flowering; in zone 9 the long warm season allows nearly year-round bloom with only a brief winter rest.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Red Drift is the red-flowered member of the Drift series of groundcover roses — a series developed by Meilland International that combines the disease resistance and landscape adaptability of groundcover roses with the repeat-blooming, dense coverage of modern landscape roses. Red Drift stays very low (12–18 inches tall, 24–36 inches wide), creating a carpet of semi-double, rich red flowers from spring through hard frost. The Drift series was specifically bred to be self-cleaning — spent flowers drop cleanly without deadheading, maintaining a tidy appearance without maintenance pruning. This self-cleaning quality combined with outstanding disease resistance (exceptional black spot and powdery mildew resistance compared to standard tea roses), heat tolerance, and continuous bloom makes Drift roses among the most low-maintenance roses available. Red Drift's low habit makes it outstanding for edging borders, planting on slopes for erosion control, ground covering under taller plants, and mass plantings where its red carpet effect and long bloom season are maximized. In zone 9, Red Drift blooms from February through December with only brief pauses; the disease resistance is maintained even in zone 9's humid summers when black spot devastates unresistant roses. A truly modern, low-maintenance landscape rose.

common_name: Sea Foam Rose (Sprawling White Shrub Rose)

planting.note: Plant container roses in late winter through spring in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile soil in full sun; Sea Foam tolerates partial shade better than most roses but flowers most abundantly in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Sea Foam is an outstanding spreading white shrub rose introduced in 1964 — a low, wide-spreading (6–8 foot spread), semi-evergreen groundcover rose producing clusters of small (1.5-inch), double, creamy-white flowers with a slight blush pink at center from late spring through fall. The sprawling, cascading habit creates an elegant weeping effect when planted on slopes, banks, and over walls where the long, flexible canes cascade downward — one of the finest rose choices for embankment plantings and flowing over retaining walls. Sea Foam has outstanding disease resistance, particularly to black spot — a nearly century-old cultivar that has proven its durability across a wide range of climates without the intensive spraying that hybrid teas require. The semi-evergreen foliage (fully evergreen in zone 9) remains attractive year-round. Sea Foam's low stature (18–36 inches tall) and wide spread make it outstanding as a groundcover, bank stabilizer, and cascading accent. Despite its groundcover habit, established plants are quite drought-tolerant and require minimal irrigation. The creamy-white flower clusters contrast beautifully with the dark-green, glossy foliage. In zone 9, Sea Foam blooms nearly continuously from March through December — one of the longest bloom seasons of any repeat-flowering rose.
